text: God blessed Noah and his sons, telling them to be fruitful and fill the earth.
Every animal would now fear mankind, given into their hands.
Every living creature would now be food, just as the green plants had been given before.
But they must not eat meat that still had its lifeblood in it.
God would demand an accounting for every human life taken, whether by animal or by man.
Whoever sheds human blood, by man his blood will be shed, for mankind is made in God's image.
God told them again to be fruitful, multiply, and fill the earth.
God spoke to Noah and his sons, establishing his covenant with them.
He promised it would extend to their descendants too.
And to every living creature that came out of the ark with them.
He promised never again to destroy all life with a flood.
God gave the rainbow as a sign of this everlasting covenant.
He set his bow in the clouds as a sign between himself and the earth.
Whenever clouds gathered and the rainbow appeared,
God would remember his covenant never to flood the earth again.
The rainbow would remind God of his everlasting covenant with every living creature.
God told Noah this was the sign of the covenant he made with all life on earth.
Noah's sons who came out of the ark were Shem, Ham, and Japheth; Ham was the father of Canaan.
From these three sons the whole earth was populated.
Noah became a farmer and planted a vineyard.
He drank some of the wine, became drunk, and lay uncovered in his tent.
Ham saw his father naked and told his two brothers outside.
Shem and Japheth took a garment and walked in backward to cover their father without looking at him.
When Noah woke and learned what his youngest son had done,
he cursed Canaan, saying he would be the lowest servant to his brothers.
He blessed the LORD, the God of Shem, and said Canaan would serve Shem.
He said God would extend Japheth's territory, and Japheth would live in Shem's tents, with Canaan as servant.
After the flood Noah lived 350 more years.
Noah lived 950 years in all, and then he died.
